topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat accusingly on the screen, stark white against the dark background of my laptop. I stared at the words—'unfortunately, we are unable to offer you the scholarship at this time'—as if they were a personal insult. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. I slammed my fist onto the desk, the cheap wood rattling a little. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my room, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing the frantic thumping in my chest. Every perfectly organized binder, every highlighted essay, suddenly seemed like a monument to my utter failure. I kicked at a discarded textbook, the frustration boiling over into a hot, stinging sensation behind my eyes. I wanted to scream, to tear the rejection letter to shreds, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a raw, shaky exhale.

[story 2]

"No. Absolutely not." I whispered the words into the quiet apartment, the sound ragged and brittle. I paced the length of the living room three times, stopping abruptly by the window and staring out at the indifferent city lights. My hands were clenched into fists so tight my knuckles were white, the skin prickling with an almost painful tension. I turned back to the desk, my breath coming in short, sharp gasps. I picked up the application packet, running a finger over the polished cover, a bitter taste rising in my mouth. It felt like a deliberate slight, a dismissal of everything I had poured into those pages. I began to furiously cross out sections of my personal statement with a red pen, the strokes jagged and violent, each line a physical manifestation of the churning turmoil inside me.

[story 3]

I couldn't look at the screen again. My gaze was fixed on the ceiling, tracing the cracks in the paint as if searching for an explanation that simply didn't exist. A low, continuous hum of irritation vibrated through my whole body. I walked to the kitchen, grabbed the nearest glass, and without thinking, I hurled it against the cupboard. It shattered with a sharp crack, and the sudden, loud noise seemed to momentarily clear the suffocating pressure in my chest. I stood there, breathing heavily, my heart hammering a desperate rhythm against my ribs. The injustice of it all felt immense, a heavy weight pressing down on my shoulders, demanding an immediate, visceral reaction. I clenched my teeth until a dull ache spread across my face, refusing to let the feeling dissipate.
